## [00:00 - 03:00]

This is the pubcast shot now what I'm John Lumer let's go. When I logged into Ads Manager today I noticed a new alert at the top. Guess what? I have been selected for an advertising study. Yeah I felt special. So the alert read this ad account has been selected to participate in a nine week study to help improve campaign performance. A small proportion of ads using custom audiences will be delivered to people beyond the custom audience. Expanding your audience is often an effective way to improve results but you can opt out. So next to this message was a button to opt out. But would I opt out? No. So first, yeah kind of cool to be part of this study. And second, oh crap really what is this? What this is is a continuation of a trend. So first there was detailed targeting expansion which became advantage detailed targeting. Then there was look-alike expansion which became advantage look-alikes. In each case the approach was clear. You picked a certain audience to target. However, if Facebook slash meta think you can get better results the audience will be expanded. That is precisely what's happening with custom audiences now at least with this study. The problem which I keep making regarding expansion of detailed targeting and look-alikes is we just have to take Facebook's word for it. Technically the audience will only be expanded if and when you can get more or better results. So in theory it can only be used for your benefit. But at least at the moment there's no way to confirm that it was actually helpful. You can't break down your results so that you can see how much your audience was expanded. Or how many conversions happened because your audience was expanded. Or the overall impact of expansion on your results. So because of this we just have to trust the expansion and that just doesn't feel right. So it's all part of this continued trend related to meta advantage. More automation, less control, more trust in Facebook, more hidden behind the curtain. I don't know if I want that with my custom audiences now. So one of the best ways to stay on top of all things Facebook and meta advertising is to subscribe to my newsletter. Go to JohnLimmer.com slash newsletter, fill out the form and I'll notify you whenever there's something important that you need to know about in the world of Facebook and meta advertising. That's JohnLimmer.com slash newsletter. Thanks again for joining me today. Until next time, do awesome things. I'm out.
